sections.heading: These guidelines apply to competent authorities and to (i) alternative investment fund
sections.title: Applicable entities for cloud outsourcing guidelines
sections.summary: These guidelines apply to competent authorities and specific financial entities including alternative investment fund managers, depositaries, UCITS management companies, central counterparties, trade repositories, investment firms, credit institutions, central securities depositories, credit rating agencies, and securitisation repositories. The scope explicitly lists entities that must comply with cloud outsourcing arrangements under these guidelines.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines which financial institutions must adhere to cloud outsourcing requirements for data transfers, impacting cross-border data processing when entities operate internationally.

requirements.text: These guidelines apply to competent authorities and alternative investment fund managers
These guidelines apply to depositaries of alternative investment funds
These guidelines apply to UCITS management companies
These guidelines apply to depositaries of UCITS
These guidelines apply to investment companies without designated management companies
These guidelines apply to central counterparties including Tier 2 third-country CCPs
These guidelines apply to trade repositories
These guidelines apply to investment firms and credit institutions for investment services
These guidelines apply to data reporting services providers
These guidelines apply to market operators of trading venues
These guidelines apply to central securities depositories
These guidelines apply to credit rating agencies
These guidelines apply to securitisation repositories
These guidelines apply to administrators of critical benchmarks

sections.source_quote: These guidelines apply to competent authorities and to (i) alternative investment fund managers (AIFMs) and depositaries of alternative investment funds (AIFs), (ii) undertakings for collective investment in transferable securities (UCITS), management companies and depositaries of UCITS, and investment companies that have not designated a management company authorised pursuant to UCITS Directive (iii) central counterparties (CCPs), including Tier 2 third-country CCPs which comply with the relevant EMIR requirements, (iv) trade repositories (TRs), (v) investment firms and credit institutions when carrying out investment services and activities, data reporting services providers and market operators of trading venues, (vi) central securities depositories (CSDs), (vii) credit rating agencies (CRAs), (viii) securitisation repositories (SRs), and (ix) administrators of critical benchmarks.

sections.heading: ESMA will also take these guidelines into account when assessing the extent to which
sections.title: Tier 2 CCP compliance with EMIR requirements
sections.summary: ESMA will consider compliance with comparable third-country requirements when assessing whether Tier 2 third-country central counterparties satisfy EMIR requirements. This section addresses the applicability of EMIR compliance for cross-border CCPs.
sections.dtia_summary: Relevant for cross-border data transfers involving central counterparties, as it specifies how third-country CCPs can meet EMIR obligations through local compliance.

requirements.text: ESMA will take into account compliance with comparable requirements in third countries for Tier 2 third-country CCPs

sections.source_quote: ESMA will also take these guidelines into account when assessing the extent to which compliance with the relevant EMIR requirements by a Tier 2 third-country CCP is satisfied by its compliance with comparable requirements in the third country pursuant to Article 25(2b)(a) of EMIR.

sections.heading: These guidelines apply in relation to the following provisions:
sections.title: Applicable regulatory provisions for cloud outsourcing
sections.summary: These guidelines apply to specific provisions from AIFMD, UCITS Directive, EMIR, SFTR, MiFID II, and other regulations. The section lists the regulatory frameworks that must be considered when implementing cloud outsourcing arrangements.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines which regulatory provisions govern cloud outsourcing, impacting data transfers and processing across different financial jurisdictions.

requirements.text: These guidelines apply to Articles 15, 18, 20 and 21(8) of AIFMD
These guidelines apply to Articles 13, 22, 38, 39, 40, 44, 45, 57(1)(d), 57(2), 57(3), 58, 75, 76, 77, 79, 81, 82 and 98 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2013/231
These guidelines apply to Articles 12(1)(a), 13, 14(1)(c), 22, 22a, 23(2), 30 and 31 of UCITS Directive
These guidelines apply to Articles 4(1) to 4(3), 4(5), 5(2), 7, 9, 23(4), 32, 38, 39 and 40 of Commission Directive 2010/43/EU
These guidelines apply to Articles 2(2)(j), 3(1), 13(2), 15, 16 and 22 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 2016/449
These guidelines apply to Articles 25(2a) and 25(2b)(a) of EMIR
These guidelines apply to Articles 2(2) of EMIR and Article 3(1) of SFTR
These guidelines apply to Articles 4(1)(1) of MiFID II and Article 4(1)(27) of MiFID II
These guidelines apply to Articles 4(1)(63) of MiFID II
These guidelines apply to Articles 4(1)(24) of MiFID II
These guidelines apply to Articles 2(1)(1) of CSDR
These guidelines apply to Articles 3(1)(b) of CRA Regulation
These guidelines apply to Articles 2(23) of SECR
These guidelines apply to Article 3(1)(25) of Benchmarks Regulation

sections.source_quote: These guidelines apply to the following regulatory provisions: Articles 15, 18, 20 and 21(8) of AIFMD; Articles 13, 22, 38, 39, 40, 44, 45, 57(1)(d), 57(2), 57(3), 58, 75, 76, 77, 79, 81, 82 and 98 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2013/231; Articles 12(1)(a), 13, 14(1)(c), 22, 22a, 23(2), 30 and 31 of UCITS Directive; Articles 4(1) to 4(3), 4(5), 5(2), 7, 9, 23(4), 32, 38, 39 and 40 of Commission Directive 2010/43/EU; Articles 2(2)(j), 3(1), 13(2), 15, 16 and 22 of Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) No 2016/449; Articles 25(2a) and 25(2b)(a) of EMIR; Articles 2(2) of EMIR and Article 3(1) of SFTR; Articles 4(1)(1) of MiFID II and Article 4(1)(27) of MiFID II; Articles 4(1)(63) of MiFID II; Articles 4(1)(24) of MiFID II; Articles 2(1)(1) of CSDR; Articles 3(1)(b) of CRA Regulation; Articles 2(23) of SECR; and Article 3(1)(25) of Benchmarks Regulation.

sections.heading: These guidelines apply from 31 July 2021 to all cloud outsourcing arrangements
sections.title: Effective date and scope of cloud outsourcing guidelines
sections.summary: These guidelines apply from 31 July 2021 to all cloud outsourcing arrangements. The scope includes specific financial entities that must comply with cloud service provider arrangements.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets the effective date and scope for cloud outsourcing, impacting data transfers and processing across financial institutions.

requirements.text: These guidelines apply from 31 July 2021 to all cloud outsourcing arrangements

sections.source_quote: These guidelines apply from 31 July 2021 to all cloud outsourcing arrangements (cont.)

sections.heading: These guidelines apply from 31 July 2021 to all cloud outsourcing arrangements (cont.)
These guidelines are based on Article 16(1) of the ESMA Regulation. The objectives
sections.title: Purpose of cloud outsourcing guidelines
sections.summary: These guidelines aim to establish consistent supervisory practices within the European System of Financial Supervision and ensure uniform application of requirements for cloud outsourcing arrangements. The objectives include identifying, addressing, and monitoring risks associated with cloud outsourcing.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines the purpose of cloud outsourcing guidelines, emphasizing risk management and consistent application of data transfer requirements across financial entities.

requirements.text: These guidelines aim to establish consistent, efficient and effective supervisory practices within the European System of Financial Supervision
These guidelines aim to ensure common, uniform and consistent application of requirements for cloud outsourcing
These guidelines aim to help firms identify, address and monitor risks from cloud outsourcing arrangements

sections.source_quote: These guidelines are based on Article 16(1) of the ESMA Regulation. The objectives of these guidelines are to establish consistent, efficient and effective supervisory practices within the European System of Financial Supervision (ESFS) and to ensure the common, uniform and consistent application of the requirements referred to in Section 1.1 under the heading 'What?' where firms outsource to CSPs. In particular, these guidelines aim to help firms and competent authorities identify, address and monitor the risks and challenges arising from cloud outsourcing arrangements, from making the decision to outsource, selecting a cloud service provider, monitoring outsourced activities to providing for exit strategies.

sections.heading: Competent authorities to which these guidelines apply should comply by incorporating
sections.title: Competent authorities must incorporate cloud guidelines into national frameworks
sections.summary: Competent authorities must integrate these cloud service provider guidelines into their national legal and supervisory frameworks. They must ensure compliance through ongoing supervision, particularly for firms targeted by specific guidelines. This applies to authorities overseeing financial institutions and cloud service providers within the EU.
sections.dtia_summary: Competent authorities must ensure national frameworks align with cloud guidelines to facilitate cross-border cloud service compliance. This affects data transfer assessments for cloud providers operating in multiple jurisdictions.

requirements.text: Competent authorities must incorporate these guidelines into national legal and supervisory frameworks
Competent authorities must ensure firms comply with guidelines through supervision

sections.source_quote: 7. Competent authorities to which these guidelines apply should comply by incorporating them into their national legal and/or supervisory frameworks as appropriate, including where particular guidelines are directed primarily at firms. In this case, competent authorities should ensure, through their supervision, that firms comply with the guidelines.

sections.heading: Through its ongoing direct supervision, ESMA will assess the application of these
sections.title: ESMA supervises cloud providers and data reporting services
sections.summary: ESMA directly supervises cloud service providers, trading repositories, trading venues, and tier 2 third-country cloud providers to assess compliance with these guidelines. From January 2022, ESMA also supervises data reporting services and administrators of EU critical benchmarks. This ensures oversight of entities handling financial data across borders.
sections.dtia_summary: ESMA's direct supervision of cloud providers and data reporting services impacts cross-border data transfers by requiring adherence to specific cloud governance standards for financial data.

requirements.text: ESMA will assess the application of these guidelines by CRAs, TRs, SRs, Tier 2 third-country CCPs
ESMA will assess the application of these guidelines by data reporting services providers and administrators of EU critical benchmarks from 1 January 2022

sections.source_quote: 8. Through its ongoing direct supervision, ESMA will assess the application of these guidelines by CRAs, TRs, SRs, Tier 2 third-country CCPs and, from 1 January 2022, data reporting services providers and administrators of EU critical benchmarks.

sections.heading: Within two months of the date of publication of the guidelines on ESMA's website in all
sections.title: Competent authorities must notify ESMA of compliance status within two months
sections.summary: Competent authorities must notify ESMA within two months of the guidelines' publication in EU languages whether they comply with the guidelines, intend to comply but do not yet, or do not comply and have no intention to comply. This notification is required for all authorities that apply these guidelines to ensure transparency in compliance status.
sections.dtia_summary: Competent authorities must report compliance status to ESMA within two months, which affects data transfer assessments by ensuring authorities are aware of non-compliance risks for cloud providers.

requirements.text: Competent authorities must notify ESMA within two months of publication in EU languages whether they comply, intend to comply but do not yet, or do not comply and have no intention to comply

sections.source_quote: 9. Within two months of the date of publication of the guidelines on ESMA's website in all EU official languages, competent authorities to which these guidelines apply must notify ESMA whether they (i) comply, (ii) do not comply but intend to comply, or (iii) do not comply and do not intend to comply with the guidelines.

sections.heading: In case of non-compliance, competent authorities must also notify ESMA within two
sections.title: Competent authorities must explain non-compliance reasons to ESMA
sections.summary: If competent authorities do not comply with the guidelines, they must notify ESMA within two months of publication in EU languages with reasons for non-compliance. This includes using a template provided by ESMA to document the reasons, ensuring transparency and accountability for non-compliant authorities.
sections.dtia_summary: Competent authorities must provide detailed reasons for non-compliance to ESMA, which impacts data transfer assessments by identifying jurisdictional gaps in cloud service compliance.

requirements.text: Competent authorities must notify ESMA within two months of publication in EU languages of reasons for non-compliance
Competent authorities must use ESMA's template to document non-compliance reasons

sections.source_quote: 10. In case of non-compliance, competent authorities must also notify ESMA within two months of the date of publication of the guidelines on ESMA's website in all EU official languages of their reasons for not complying with the guidelines. A template for notifications is available on ESMA's website. Once the template has been filled in, it shall be transmitted to ESMA.

sections.heading: Firms are not required to report whether they comply with these guidelines.
sections.title: Firms not required to report compliance with cloud guidelines
sections.summary: Firms are not required to report whether they comply with these cloud service provider guidelines. This means firms have no obligation to inform ESMA or competent authorities about their compliance status with the guidelines, reducing reporting burdens for financial institutions.
sections.dtia_summary: Firms do not need to report compliance with cloud guidelines, which simplifies data transfer assessments by removing reporting obligations for cloud service providers.

requirements.text: Firms are not required to report compliance with these guidelines

sections.source_quote: 11. Firms are not required to report whether they comply with these guidelines.

sections.heading: A firm should have a defined and up-to-date cloud outsourcing strategy that is
sections.title: Firms must have updated cloud outsourcing strategy
sections.summary: Firms must maintain a defined and up-to-date cloud outsourcing strategy that aligns with their internal policies, information security practices, and operational risk management frameworks. This ensures cloud services meet regulatory standards and protect financial data.
sections.dtia_summary: Firms must have a cloud outsourcing strategy that meets internal security and risk management standards, which is critical for data transfer impact assessments involving cloud services.

requirements.text: Firms must have a defined and up-to-date cloud outsourcing strategy
Cloud outsourcing strategy must be consistent with firm's internal policies and processes
Cloud outsourcing strategy must align with information and communication technology, information security, and operational risk management

sections.source_quote: 12. A firm should have a defined and up-to-date cloud outsourcing strategy that is consistent with the firm's relevant strategies and internal policies and processes, including in relation to information and communication technology, information security, and operational risk management.

sections.heading: A firm should:
sections.title: Cloud outsourcing responsibility assignment and oversight
sections.summary: Firms must clearly assign responsibilities for cloud outsourcing documentation, management, and control within their organization. They must allocate sufficient resources to ensure compliance with guidelines and all applicable legal requirements. Firms should establish an oversight function or designate senior staff accountable to management for monitoring cloud outsourcing risks, considering business scale, complexity, and financial system risks. Small firms must ensure clear task division for oversight.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates clear oversight of cloud outsourcing arrangements to ensure risks are managed appropriately for data transfers across borders, particularly for critical functions.

requirements.text: Clearly assign responsibilities for documentation, management and control of cloud outsourcing arrangements
Allocate sufficient resources to ensure compliance with guidelines and legal requirements
Establish cloud outsourcing oversight function or designate senior staff accountable to management
Consider business nature, scale, complexity and financial system risks when managing cloud outsourcing
Ensure management body has technical skills to understand cloud outsourcing risks
Small firms must ensure clear division of tasks and responsibilities for oversight

sections.source_quote: 13. A firm should: a) clearly assign the responsibilities for the documentation, management and control of cloud outsourcing arrangements within its organisation; b) allocate sufficient resources to ensure compliance with these guidelines and all of the legal requirements applicable to its cloud outsourcing arrangements; c) establish a cloud outsourcing oversight function or designate senior staff members who are directly accountable to the management body and responsible for managing and overseeing the risks of cloud outsourcing arrangements.

sections.heading: A firm should monitor the performance of activities, the security measures and the
sections.title: Cloud outsourcing performance and security monitoring
sections.summary: Firms must monitor CSP performance, security measures, and adherence to service levels on a risk-based approach, prioritizing critical or important outsourced functions. This monitoring ensures ongoing compliance with cloud outsourcing arrangements and helps identify potential security or operational issues.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires firms to monitor cloud service providers' performance and security to ensure data transfers remain secure and compliant across borders.

requirements.text: Monitor performance of activities, security measures and adherence to agreed service levels by CSPs

sections.source_quote: 14. A firm should monitor the performance of activities, the security measures and the adherence to agreed service levels by its CSPs. This monitoring should be risk-based, with a primary focus on the critical or important functions that have been outsourced.

sections.heading: A firm should reassess whether its cloud outsourcing arrangements concern a critical
sections.title: Periodic reassessment of critical cloud functions
sections.summary: Firms must periodically reassess whether cloud outsourcing arrangements concern critical or important functions, and trigger reassessment whenever the risk, nature, or scale of an outsourced function changes materially. This ensures ongoing alignment with evolving business needs and risks.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires regular reassessment of cloud functions to ensure critical data transfers remain appropriately safeguarded across borders.

requirements.text: Periodically reassess whether cloud outsourcing arrangements concern critical or important functions
Reassess whenever risk, nature or scale of outsourced function has materially changed

sections.source_quote: 15. A firm should reassess whether its cloud outsourcing arrangements concern a critical or important function periodically and whenever the risk, nature or scale of an outsourced function has materially changed.

sections.heading: A firm should maintain an updated register of information on all its cloud outsourcing
sections.title: Cloud outsourcing register maintenance
sections.summary: Firms must maintain an updated register of all cloud outsourcing arrangements, distinguishing between critical/important and non-critical functions. The register must include reasons for classification, and maintain records of terminated arrangements for an appropriate period, considering national law.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates detailed documentation of cloud data transfers to ensure compliance with cross-border data transfer requirements.

requirements.text: Maintain updated register of information on all cloud outsourcing arrangements
Distinguish between critical/important and other outsourcing arrangements
Provide brief summary of reasons why outsourced function is critical or important
Maintain record of terminated cloud outsourcing arrangements for appropriate time period

sections.source_quote: 16. A firm should maintain an updated register of information on all its cloud outsourcing arrangements, distinguishing between the outsourcing of critical or important functions and other outsourcing arrangements. When distinguishing between the outsourcing of critical or important functions and other outsourcing arrangements, it should provide a brief summary of the reasons why the outsourced function is or is not considered critical or important. Taking into account national law, a firm should also maintain a record of terminated cloud outsourcing arrangements for an appropriate time period.

sections.heading: For the cloud outsourcing arrangements concerning critical or important functions, the
sections.title: Critical cloud outsourcing register details
sections.summary: For critical cloud outsourcing arrangements, firms must include specific details in their register: reference numbers, dates, outsourced function descriptions, data types, criticality status, CSP information, data storage locations, risk assessment dates, and approval details. This ensures comprehensive tracking of high-risk data transfers.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies detailed requirements for registers of critical cloud data transfers to ensure robust cross-border data protection.

requirements.text: Include reference number for each cloud outsourcing arrangement
Provide start date and renewal dates for CSP and firm
Describe outsourced function including data types and whether personal data is involved
Assign category reflecting nature of outsourced function
Indicate whether outsourced function supports time-critical business operations
Provide CSP name, brand, country, registration details, address and parent company
Specify governing law and jurisdiction choice
Detail cloud services, deployment models and data storage locations
Record date of most recent criticality assessment and next planned assessment
Include date of most recent CSP risk assessment with summary and next planned date
Identify individual or decision-making body that approved the arrangement
List sub-outsourcers for critical functions including locations and data storage
Provide estimated annual budget cost of cloud outsourcing arrangement

sections.source_quote: 17. For the cloud outsourcing arrangements concerning critical or important functions, the register should include at least the following information for each cloud outsourcing arrangement: a) a reference number; b) the start date and, as applicable, the next contract renewal date, the end date and/or notice periods for the CSP and for the firm; c) a brief description of the outsourced function, including the data that is outsourced and whether this data includes personal data (for example by providing a yes or no in a separate data field); d) a category assigned by the firm that reflects the nature of the outsourced function (for example information technology function, control function), which should facilitate the identification of the different types of cloud outsourcing arrangements; e) whether the outsourced function supports business operations that are time-critical; f) the name and the brand name (if any) of the CSP, its country of registration, its corporate registration number, its legal entity identifier (where available), its registered address, its relevant contact details and the name of its parent company (if any); g) the governing law of the cloud outsourcing arrangement and, if any, the choice of jurisdiction; h) the type of cloud services and deployment models and the specific nature of the data to be held and the locations (namely regions or countries) where such data may be stored; i) the date of the most recent assessment of the criticality or importance of the outsourced function and the date of the next planned assessment; j) the date of the most recent risk assessment/audit of the CSP together with a brief summary of the main results, and the date of the next planned risk assessment/audit; k) the individual or decision-making body in the firm that approved the cloud outsourcing arrangement; l) where applicable, the names of any sub-outsourcer to which a critical or important function (or material parts thereof) is sub-outsourced, including the countries where the sub-outsourcers are registered, where the sub-outsourced service will be performed, and the locations (namely regions or countries) where the data will be stored; m) the estimated annual budget cost of the cloud outsourcing arrangement.

sections.heading: For the cloud outsourcing arrangements concerning non-critical or non-important
sections.title: Non-critical cloud outsourcing register details
sections.summary: For non-critical cloud outsourcing arrangements, firms must define the register details based on the nature, scale, and complexity of risks inherent to the outsourced function. This ensures appropriate level of detail for lower-risk cloud activities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies that non-critical cloud data transfers require less detailed documentation than critical transfers, reducing DTIA complexity for lower-risk scenarios.

requirements.text: Define information to include in register based on nature, scale and complexity of risks

sections.source_quote: 18. For the cloud outsourcing arrangements concerning non-critical or non-important functions, a firm should define the information to be included in the register based on the nature, scale and complexity of the risks inherent to the outsourced function.

sections.heading: Before entering into any cloud outsourcing arrangement, a firm should:
sections.title: Pre-cloud outsourcing risk assessment requirements
sections.summary: Firms must assess if cloud outsourcing affects critical functions, identify all relevant risks, conduct due diligence on prospective cloud service providers (CSPs), and evaluate potential conflicts of interest before entering any cloud outsourcing arrangement. These obligations apply to financial institutions and firms subject to the ESMA guidelines.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires firms to conduct comprehensive risk assessments before cloud outsourcing, particularly for critical functions, to ensure adequate safeguards for data security and operational continuity across borders.

requirements.text: Assess if cloud outsourcing arrangement concerns critical or important function
Identify and assess all relevant risks of cloud outsourcing arrangement
Undertake appropriate due diligence on prospective cloud service provider
Identify and assess any conflict of interest arising from outsourcing

sections.source_quote: Before entering into any cloud outsourcing arrangement, a firm should: a) assess if the cloud outsourcing arrangement concerns a critical or important function; b) identify and assess all relevant risks of the cloud outsourcing arrangement; c) undertake appropriate due diligence on the prospective CSP; d) identify and assess any conflict of interest that the outsourcing may cause.

sections.heading: The pre-outsourcing analysis and due diligence on the prospective CSP should be
sections.title: Proportionate pre-outsourcing due diligence requirements
sections.summary: Firms must ensure pre-outsourcing analysis and due diligence on cloud service providers (CSPs) are proportionate to the function's nature, scale, complexity, and inherent risks. This includes assessing impacts on operational, legal, compliance, and reputational risks to maintain adequate safeguards for critical functions.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates that due diligence must be proportionate to the function's risk profile, ensuring that cloud outsourcing arrangements do not compromise financial institution security or compliance across borders.

requirements.text: Pre-outsourcing analysis and due diligence on CSP should be proportionate to function's nature, scale, complexity and inherent risks
Include assessment of potential impact on operational, legal, compliance and reputational risks

sections.source_quote: The pre-outsourcing analysis and due diligence on the prospective CSP should be proportionate to the nature, scale and complexity of the function that the firm intends to outsource and the risks inherent to this function. It should include at least an assessment of the potential impact of the cloud outsourcing arrangement on the firm's operational, legal, compliance, and reputational risks.

sections.heading: In case the cloud outsourcing arrangement concerns critical or important functions, a
sections.title: Enhanced risk assessment for critical cloud functions
sections.summary: Firms must conduct detailed risk assessments for cloud outsourcing arrangements involving critical functions, covering technology, security, business continuity, legal compliance, reputational risks, operational risks, and oversight limitations. This includes evaluating impacts on data portability, political stability, and cross-border data transfers to ensure compliance with GDPR.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires comprehensive risk analysis for critical functions, including cross-border data transfer implications and political stability assessments, to ensure GDPR compliance in cloud outsourcing arrangements.

requirements.text: Assess all relevant risks arising from cloud outsourcing arrangement, including information security, business continuity, legal compliance, reputational risks, operational risks and oversight limitations
Evaluate risks related to selected cloud service, deployment models, migration processes, data sensitivity and security measures
Assess risks from interoperability of systems and applications with CSP
Evaluate data portability capacity to transfer data between CSPs or back to firm
Assess political stability, security situation, legal system, insolvency laws, data protection laws and GDPR transfer conditions of countries where data is stored
Evaluate concentration risks from multiple firms using same CSP or sub-outsourcing chains
Weigh expected benefits and costs of cloud outsourcing against significant risks

sections.source_quote: In case the cloud outsourcing arrangement concerns critical or important functions, a firm should also: a) assess all relevant risks that may arise as a result of the cloud outsourcing arrangement, including risks in relation to information and communication technology, information security, business continuity, legal and compliance, reputational risks, operational risks, and possible oversight limitations for the firm, arising from: i. the selected cloud service and the proposed deployment models; ii. the migration and/or the implementation processes; iii. the sensitivity of the function and the related data which are under consideration to be outsourced and the security measures which would need to be taken; iv. the interoperability of the systems and applications of the firm and the CSP, namely their capacity to exchange information and mutually use the information that has been exchanged; v. the portability of the data of the firm, namely the capacity to easily transfer the firm's data from one CSP to another or back to the firm; vi. the political stability, the security situation and the legal system (including the law enforcement provisions in place, the insolvency law provisions that would apply in case of the CSP's bankruptcy, the laws on data protection in force and whether the conditions for transfer of personal data to a third country under the GDPR are met) of the countries (within or outside the EU) where the outsourced functions would be provided and where the outsourced data would be stored; in case of sub-outsourcing, the additional risks that may arise if the sub-outsourcer is located in a third country or a different country from the CSP and, in case of a sub-outsourcing chain, any additional risk which may arise, including in relation to the absence of a direct contract between the firm and the sub-outsourcer performing the outsourced function; vii. possible concentration within the firm (including, where applicable, at the level of its group,) caused by multiple cloud outsourcing arrangements with the same CSP as well as possible concentration within the EU financial sector, caused by multiple firms making use of the same CSP or a small group of CSPs. When assessing the concentration risk, the firm should take into account all its cloud outsourcing arrangements (and, where applicable, the cloud outsourcing arrangements at the level of its group) with that CSP; b) take into account the expected benefits and costs of the cloud outsourcing arrangement, including weighing any significant risks which may be reduced or better managed against any significant risks which may arise as a result of the cloud outsourcing arrangement.

sections.heading: In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, the due diligence should include
sections.title: CSP suitability evaluation for critical functions
sections.summary: Firms must evaluate CSP suitability for critical functions by assessing business reputation, skills, resources, organizational structure, and relevant authorizations. This includes evaluating information security practices, service support, and business continuity/disaster recovery plans to ensure reliable and professional service delivery.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires firms to verify CSP capabilities and security measures to ensure critical functions remain compliant with GDPR when outsourced to cloud services across borders.

requirements.text: Ensure CSP has business reputation, skills, resources (human, IT, financial), organizational structure and relevant authorizations to perform critical functions reliably
Assess CSP's management of information security and protection of personal, confidential or sensitive data
Evaluate CSP's service support including support plans and contacts, and incident management processes
Assess CSP's business continuity and disaster recovery plans

sections.source_quote: In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, the due diligence should include an evaluation of the suitability of the CSP. When assessing the suitability of the CSP, a firm should ensure that the CSP has the business reputation, the skills, the resources (including human, IT and financial), the organisational structure and, if applicable, the relevant authorisation(s) or registration(s) to perform the critical or important function in a reliable and professional manner and to meet its obligations over the duration of the cloud outsourcing arrangement. Additional factors to be considered in the due diligence on the CSP include, but are not limited to: a) the management of information security and in particular the protection of personal, confidential or otherwise sensitive data; b) the service support, including support plans and contacts, and incident management processes; c) the business continuity and disaster recovery plans;

sections.heading: Where appropriate and in order to support the due diligence performed, a firm may also
sections.title: Certification and audit support for due diligence
sections.summary: Firms may use international standards certifications and external or internal audit reports to support their pre-outsourcing due diligence, provided such measures are appropriate for the specific cloud outsourcing arrangement.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows firms to leverage third-party certifications and audits to validate cloud service provider compliance, enhancing the robustness of data transfer impact assessments for critical functions.

requirements.text: Use certifications based on international standards and external or internal audit reports to support due diligence

sections.source_quote: Where appropriate and in order to support the due diligence performed, a firm may also use certifications based on international standards and external or internal audit reports.

sections.heading: If a firm becomes aware of significant deficiencies and/or significant changes to the
sections.title: Review due diligence after significant CSP changes
sections.summary: Firms must promptly review or re-perform pre-outsourcing analysis and due diligence on CSPs if they become aware of significant deficiencies or changes to services provided by the CSP or the CSP's situation.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates ongoing monitoring of CSP performance and compliance, ensuring that data transfer impact assessments remain current and effective after significant changes to cloud service providers.

requirements.text: Promptly review or re-perform pre-outsourcing analysis and due diligence on CSP when significant deficiencies or changes to services or CSP situation are identified

sections.source_quote: If a firm becomes aware of significant deficiencies and/or significant changes to the services provided or to the situation of the CSP, the pre-outsourcing analysis and due diligence on the CSP should be promptly reviewed or where needed re-performed.

sections.heading: In case a firm enters into a new arrangement or renews an existing arrangement with
sections.title: Risk-based due diligence for re-assessed CSPs
sections.summary: When a firm renews an existing arrangement with a CSP that has already been assessed, it must determine on a risk-based approach whether new due diligence is required. This applies to firms outsourcing critical functions to cloud service providers. The assessment must consider the specific risks associated with the renewed arrangement to ensure ongoing compliance with data protection standards.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires firms to reassess cloud provider risks before renewing agreements to ensure continued data protection compliance without unnecessary disruption.

requirements.text: Determine on a risk-based approach whether new due diligence is needed

sections.source_quote: 25. In case a firm enters into a new arrangement or renews an existing arrangement with a CSP that has already been assessed, it should determine, on a risk-based approach, whether a new due diligence is needed.

sections.heading: In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, the written agreement should
sections.title: Minimum contractual terms for cloud outsourcing
sections.summary: When outsourcing critical or important functions, firms must include specific contractual terms in written agreements with CSPs. These terms cover function description, agreement duration, governing law, financial obligations, sub-outsourcing rules, data location, security provisions, performance monitoring rights, service levels, reporting obligations, incident management, insurance requirements, business continuity plans, and audit rights. The clauses ensure transparency and accountability in cloud service arrangements.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines mandatory contractual terms for cloud providers to ensure data security, compliance, and operational continuity during outsourcing activities.

requirements.text: Include a clear description of the outsourced function
Specify start and end dates of the agreement and notice periods
State governing law and jurisdiction choice
Detail financial obligations of both parties
Define sub-outsourcing permissions and conditions
Specify data processing and storage locations and change notification requirements
Include information security and data protection provisions
Grant firm rights to monitor CSP performance regularly
Set agreed service levels with performance targets
Define CSP reporting obligations to the firm
Include incident management provisions and timely reporting
Specify mandatory insurance requirements and coverage levels
Require business continuity and disaster recovery plans
Grant firm access rights to inspect CSP systems and data
Ensure data can be accessed, recovered, and returned to the firm

sections.source_quote: 28. In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, the written agreement should include at least: a) a clear description of the outsourced function; b) the start date and end date, where applicable, of the agreement and the notice periods for the CSP and for the firm; c) the governing law of the agreement and, if any, the choice of jurisdiction; d) the firm's and the CSP's financial obligations; e) whether sub-outsourcing is permitted, and, if so, under which conditions, having regard to Guideline 7; f) the location(s) (namely regions or countries) where the outsourced function will be provided and where data will be processed and stored, and the conditions to be met, including a requirement to notify the firm if the CSP proposes to change the location(s); g) provisions regarding information security and protection of personal data, having regard to Guideline 4; h) the right for the firm to monitor the CSP's performance under the cloud outsourcing arrangement on a regular basis, having regard to Guideline 6; i) the agreed service levels, which should include, quantitative and qualitative performance targets in order to allow for timely monitoring so that appropriate corrective actions can be taken without undue delay if agreed service levels are not met; j) the reporting obligations of the CSP to the firm and, as appropriate, the obligations to submit reports relevant for the firm's security function and key functions, such as reports prepared by the internal audit function of the CSP; k) provisions regarding the management of incidents by the CSP, including the obligation for the CSP to report to the firm without undue delay incidents that have affected the operation of the firm's contracted service; l) whether the CSP should take mandatory insurance against certain risks and, if applicable, the level of insurance cover requested; m) the requirements for the CSP to implement and test business continuity and disaster recovery plans; n) the requirement for the CSP to grant the firm, its competent authorities and any other person appointed by the firm or the competent authorities the right to access ('access rights') and to inspect ('audit rights') the relevant information, premises, systems and devices of the CSP to the extent necessary to monitor the CSP's performance under the cloud outsourcing arrangement and its compliance with the applicable regulatory and contractual requirements, having regard to Guideline 6; o) provisions to ensure that the data that the CSP processes or stores on behalf of the firm can be accessed, recovered and returned to the firm as needed, having regard to Guideline 5.

sections.heading: A firm should set information security requirements in its internal policies and
sections.title: Ongoing information security monitoring
sections.summary: Firms must establish information security requirements in internal policies and procedures, and within cloud outsourcing agreements. These requirements must be proportionate to the function's nature, scale, complexity, and associated risks. Firms must continuously monitor compliance with these security measures to protect confidential, personal, and sensitive data.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates continuous monitoring of information security to ensure data protection during cloud outsourcing, aligning with risk-based approaches.

requirements.text: Set information security requirements in internal policies and procedures
Include information security requirements in cloud outsourcing agreements
Monitor compliance with security requirements on an ongoing basis
Ensure requirements are proportionate to function nature, scale, complexity, and risks

sections.source_quote: 29. A firm should set information security requirements in its internal policies and procedures and within the cloud outsourcing written agreement and monitor compliance with these requirements on an ongoing basis, including to protect confidential, personal or otherwise sensitive data. These requirements should be proportionate to the nature, scale and complexity of the function that the firm outsources to the CSP and the risks inherent to this function.

sections.heading: For that purpose, in case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, and without
sections.title: Information security requirements for critical functions
sections.summary: For critical or important functions, firms must implement specific information security requirements in cloud outsourcing agreements. These include clear role allocation, strong authentication mechanisms, encryption for data in transit, memory, at rest, and backups, network segregation, secure API integrations, business continuity and disaster recovery controls, data location strategies, and compliance verification with internationally recognized standards. These measures ensure robust data protection and operational resilience.
sections.dtia_summary: Specifies detailed security controls for critical cloud functions to mitigate risks and ensure data integrity during outsourcing.

requirements.text: Ensure clear allocation of information security roles and responsibilities
Implement strong authentication mechanisms and access controls
Use encryption technologies for data in transit, memory, at rest, and backups
Implement appropriate key management solutions
Ensure appropriate network availability and segregation
Secure application programming interfaces (APIs) with robust policies
Ensure effective business continuity and disaster recovery controls
Adopt a risk-based approach to data storage and processing locations
Verify CSP compliance with internationally recognized security standards

sections.source_quote: 30. For that purpose, in case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, and without prejudice to the applicable requirements under GDPR, a firm, applying a risk-based approach, should at least: a) information security organisation: ensure that there is a clear allocation of information security roles and responsibilities between the firm and the CSP, including in relation to threat detection, incident management and patch management, and ensure that the CSP is effectively able to fulfil its roles and responsibilities; b) identity and access management: ensure that strong authentication mechanisms (for example multi-factor authentication) and access controls are in place with a view to prevent unauthorised access to the firm's data and back-end cloud resources; c) encryption and key management: ensure that relevant encryption technologies are used, where necessary, for data in transit, data in memory, data at rest and data back-ups, in combination with appropriate key management solutions to limit the risk of non-authorised access to the encryption keys; in particular, the firm should consider state-of-the-art technology and processes when selecting its key management solution; d) operations and network security: consider appropriate levels of network availability, network segregation (for example tenant isolation in the shared environment of the cloud, operational separation as regards the web, application logic, operating system, network, Data Base Management System (DBMS) and storage layers) and processing environments (for example test, User Acceptance Testing, development, production); e) application programming interfaces (API): consider mechanisms for the integration of the cloud services with the systems of the firm to ensure security of APIs (for example establishing and maintaining information security policies and procedures for APIs across multiple system interfaces, jurisdictions, and business functions to prevent unauthorised disclosure, modification or destruction of data); f) business continuity and disaster recovery: ensure that effective business continuity and disaster recovery controls are in place (for example by setting minimum capacity requirements, selecting hosting options that are geographically spread, with the capability to switch from one to the other, or requesting and reviewing documentation showing the transport route of the firm's data among the CSP's systems, as well as considering the possibility to replicate machine images to an independent storage location, which is sufficiently isolated from the network or taken offline); g) data location: adopt a risk-based approach to data storage and data processing location(s) (namely regions or countries); h) compliance & monitoring: verify that the CSP complies with internationally recognised information security standards and has implemented appropriate information security controls (for example by requesting the CSP to provide evidence that it conducts relevant information security reviews and by performing regular assessments and tests on the CSP's information security arrangements).

sections.heading: In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, a firm should ensure that it is
sections.title: Exit strategy for cloud outsourcing
sections.summary: Firms must develop comprehensive exit plans for cloud outsourcing arrangements to ensure seamless transition without business disruption or compliance detriment. These plans must cover documented exit strategies, alternative solutions, transition protocols, and contractual obligations for data transfer. The exit strategy must maintain data confidentiality, integrity, and availability while complying with applicable legislation.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires firms to prepare exit strategies for cloud providers to ensure data continuity and regulatory compliance during transitions.

requirements.text: Develop comprehensive, documented, and tested exit plans
Identify alternative solutions and develop transition plans for data transfer
Ensure cloud outsourcing agreements include obligations for CSP to support orderly data transfer
Include secure data deletion from CSP and sub-outsourcers

sections.source_quote: 31. In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, a firm should ensure that it is able to exit the cloud outsourcing arrangement without undue disruption to its business activities and services to its clients, and without any detriment to its compliance with its obligations under the applicable legislation, as well as the confidentiality, integrity and availability of its data. For that purpose, a firm should: a) develop exit plans that are comprehensive, documented and sufficiently tested. These plans should be updated as needed, including in case of changes in the outsourced function; b) identify alternative solutions and develop transition plans to remove the outsourced function and data from the CSP and, where applicable, any sub-outsourcer, and transfer them to the alternative CSP indicated by the firm or directly back to the firm. These solutions should be defined with regard to the challenges that may arise from the location of the data, taking the necessary measures to ensure business continuity during the transition phase; c) ensure that the cloud outsourcing written agreement includes an obligation for the CSP to support the orderly transfer of the outsourced function, and the related processing of data, from the CSP and any sub-outsourcer to another CSP indicated by the firm or directly to the firm in case the firm activates the exit strategy. The obligation to support the orderly transfer of the outsourced function, and the related treatment of data, should include where relevant the secure deletion of the data from the systems of the CSP and any sub-outsourcer.

sections.heading: When developing the exit plans and solutions referred to in points (a) and (b) above
sections.title: Exit strategy development requirements
sections.summary: Firms must define specific elements for exit strategies, including objectives, trigger events, business impact analysis, role assignments, testing procedures, and success criteria. These elements ensure the exit strategy is practical, timely, and aligned with the firm's operational needs and regulatory obligations.
sections.dtia_summary: Details the components necessary for effective exit strategies to minimize disruption during cloud service transitions.

requirements.text: Define objectives of the exit strategy
Define trigger events for exit strategy activation
Perform business impact analysis for the outsourced function
Assign roles and responsibilities for exit strategy management
Test the exit strategy using a risk-based approach
Define success criteria for the transition

sections.source_quote: 32. When developing the exit plans and solutions referred to in points (a) and (b) above ('exit strategy'), the firm should consider the following: a) define the objectives of the exit strategy; b) define the trigger events that could activate the exit strategy. These should include at least the termination of the cloud outsourcing arrangement at the initiative of the firm or the CSP and the failure or other serious discontinuation of the business activity of the CSP; c) perform a business impact analysis that is commensurate to the function outsourced to identify what human and other resources would be required to implement the exit strategy; d) assign roles and responsibilities to manage the exit strategy; e) test the appropriateness of the exit strategy, using a risk-based approach, (for example, by carrying out an analysis of the potential costs, impact, resources and timing implications of transferring an outsourced service to an alternative provider); f) define success criteria of the transition.

sections.heading: A firm should include indicators of the trigger events of the exit strategy in its ongoing
sections.title: Exit strategy trigger events for cloud services monitoring
sections.summary: Firms must include indicators of trigger events for their exit strategy within ongoing monitoring of cloud service providers under outsourcing arrangements. These indicators are part of the firm's oversight mechanisms to ensure readiness for potential termination of the cloud service contract. The requirement applies to firms outsourcing cloud services to service providers.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires firms to define specific triggers for exiting cloud arrangements, which is critical for DTIAs to assess exit risks and ensure data portability in case of service termination.

requirements.text: Include indicators of trigger events for exit strategy in ongoing monitoring of cloud services

sections.source_quote: A firm should include indicators of the trigger events of the exit strategy in its ongoing monitoring and oversight of the services provided by the CSP under the cloud outsourcing arrangement.

sections.heading: A firm should ensure that the cloud outsourcing written agreement does not limit the
sections.title: Cloud agreements must not restrict access and audit rights
sections.summary: Firms must ensure that cloud outsourcing agreements do not restrict their ability to exercise access and audit rights, as well as oversight options over the cloud service provider. This requirement applies to firms and their competent authorities when conducting audits of cloud service providers.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures that firms retain necessary access and audit rights in cloud arrangements, which is vital for DTIAs to verify compliance with data protection and security obligations across borders.

requirements.text: Cloud outsourcing written agreement must not limit firm's and competent authority's access and audit rights

sections.source_quote: A firm should ensure that the cloud outsourcing written agreement does not limit the firm's and competent authority's effective exercise of the access and audit rights and oversight options on the CSP.

sections.heading: A firm should ensure that the exercise of the access and audit rights (for example, the
sections.title: Audit scope considerations for critical cloud functions
sections.summary: Firms must ensure that audit frequency, scope, and services audited take into account whether cloud outsourcing relates to critical or important functions, as well as the risks and impact on the firm from the cloud arrangement. This requirement applies to firms conducting audits of cloud service providers.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates that firms tailor audit scope to critical functions, which is essential for DTIAs to assess risk levels in cloud data transfers and ensure adequate oversight for sensitive data.

requirements.text: Audit frequency and scope must consider critical/important functions and associated risks

sections.source_quote: A firm should ensure that the exercise of the access and audit rights (for example, the audit frequency and the areas and services to be audited) takes into consideration whether the outsourcing is related to a critical or important function, as well as the nature and extent of the risks and impact arising from the cloud outsourcing arrangement on the firm.

sections.heading: In case the exercise of the access or audit rights, or the use of certain audit techniques
sections.title: Risk mitigation for audit activities in cloud services
sections.summary: If audit activities create risks to the cloud service provider's environment or other clients' data, the CSP must provide a rationale for the risk and agree on alternative methods to achieve similar results without compromising data confidentiality, integrity, or availability. This requirement applies to firms and CSPs during audits.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses risk mitigation for audits in cloud services, ensuring DTIAs account for potential disruptions to data security when conducting audits across borders.

requirements.text: CSP must provide rationale for risks created by audit activities and agree on alternative methods

sections.source_quote: In case the exercise of the access or audit rights, or the use of certain audit techniques create a risk for the environment of the CSP and/or another CSP's client (for example by impacting service levels, confidentiality, integrity and availability of data), the CSP should provide a clear rationale to the firm as to why this would create a risk and the CSP should agree with the firm on alternative ways to achieve a similar result (for example, the inclusion of specific controls to be tested in a specific report/certification produced by the CSP).

sections.heading: Without prejudice to their final responsibility regarding cloud outsourcing arrangements,
sections.title: Efficient audit sharing for cloud services
sections.summary: Firms may use third-party certifications, external or internal audit reports from CSPs, or pooled audits with other clients to reduce audit burden and improve efficiency. This requirement applies to firms outsourcing cloud services to CSPs.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows firms to share audit resources across clients, which is relevant for DTIAs to assess cost-effective oversight mechanisms without compromising data security in cross-border cloud arrangements.

requirements.text: Firms may use third-party certifications and external/internal audit reports from CSPs
Firms may use pooled audits with other CSP clients or third-party auditors

sections.source_quote: Firms may use: a) third-party certifications and external or internal audit reports made available by the CSP; b) pooled audits performed jointly with other clients of the same CSP or pooled audits performed by a third-party auditor appointed by multiple clients of the same CSP.

sections.heading: In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, a firm should assess whether
sections.title: Certification adequacy for critical cloud functions
sections.summary: Firms must assess whether third-party certifications and audit reports are adequate for critical or important functions, and should not rely solely on these certifications over time. This requirement applies to firms outsourcing critical cloud services.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures that firms validate the ongoing adequacy of certifications for critical cloud functions, which is crucial for DTIAs to maintain robust data protection in high-risk cross-border transfers.

requirements.text: Assess adequacy of third-party certifications for critical/important functions
Do not solely rely on certifications over time

sections.source_quote: In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, a firm should assess whether the third-party certifications and external or internal audit reports referred to in paragraph 37(a) are adequate and sufficient to comply with its obligations under the applicable legislation and should aim at not solely relying on these certifications and reports over time.

sections.heading: In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, a firm should make use of the
sections.title: Outsourcing critical functions: certification and audit requirements
sections.summary: Firms must use third-party certifications and audit reports for critical or important functions only if they verify coverage of key systems, controls, and compliance with legislation. They must regularly assess the certifications, ensure they remain current, and maintain contractual rights to expand scope or conduct on-site audits. The requirements apply to firms outsourcing critical functions to cloud service providers.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires firms to validate that cloud certifications cover critical systems and controls, ensuring ongoing relevance and contractual rights to audit. This impacts DTIA by mandating thorough verification of third-party compliance before outsourcing critical functions.

requirements.text: Verify certifications or audit reports cover CSP's key systems, processes, applications, infrastructure, data centres, and key controls
Regularly assess certifications or audit reports to ensure they are not obsolete
Ensure CSP's key systems and controls are covered in future certification or audit report versions
Be satisfied with the certifying or auditing party's qualifications, expertise, and rotation
Confirm certifications are issued and audits follow appropriate standards with effective control testing
Maintain contractual right to request scope expansion of certifications or audit reports
Retain contractual right to perform individual on-site audits at discretion

sections.source_quote: 39. In case of outsourcing of critical or important functions, a firm should make use of the third-party certifications and external or internal audit reports referred to in paragraph 37(a) only if it: a) is satisfied that the scope of the certifications or the audit reports covers the CSP's key systems (for example processes, applications, infrastructure, data centres), the key controls identified by the firm and the compliance with the relevant applicable legislation; b) thoroughly assesses the content of the certifications or audit reports on a regular basis and verify that the certifications or reports are not obsolete; c) ensures that the CSP's key systems and controls are covered in future versions of the certifications or audit reports; d) is satisfied with the certifying or auditing party (for example with regard to its qualifications, expertise, re-performance/verification of the evidence in the underlying audit file as well as rotation of the certifying or auditing company); e) is satisfied that the certifications are issued and that the audits are performed according to appropriate standards and include a test of the effectiveness of the key controls in place; f) has the contractual right to request the expansion of the scope of the certifications or audit reports to other relevant systems and controls of the CSP; the number and frequency of such requests for scope modification should be reasonable and legitimate from a risk management perspective; g) retains the contractual right to perform individual on-site audits at its discretion with regard to the outsourced function.

sections.heading: A firm should ensure that, before an on-site visit, including by a third party appointed
sections.title: On-site audits: prior notice requirements
sections.summary: Firms must provide prior written notice to cloud service providers (CSPs) at least a reasonable time before on-site visits, including those by third-party auditors. Exceptions include emergencies or crises where notice would compromise audit effectiveness. The notice must specify location, purpose, and participating personnel.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires timely notice for on-site audits, ensuring CSPs are aware of assessments before they occur. This impacts DTIA by clarifying when and how audits can be conducted without disrupting critical functions.

requirements.text: Provide CSP with prior written notice within a reasonable time period before on-site visits
Include location, purpose of visit, and participating personnel in notice

sections.source_quote: 40. A firm should ensure that, before an on-site visit, including by a third party appointed by the firm (for example an auditor), prior notice within a reasonable time period is provided to the CSP, unless an early prior notification is not possible due to an emergency or crisis situation or would lead to a situation where the audit would no longer be effective. Such notice should include the location, purpose of the visit and the personnel that will participate to the visit.

sections.heading: Considering that cloud services present a high level of technical complexity and raise
sections.title: Cloud audit staff competency requirements
sections.summary: Staff performing audits of cloud services must have the right skills and knowledge to assess cloud services effectively and conduct relevant audits. This applies to both internal auditors and third-party auditors reviewing certifications or audit reports from cloud service providers.
sections.dtia_summary: Ensures auditors have necessary expertise to evaluate cloud services, which is critical for DTIA as it affects the validity of compliance assessments.

requirements.text: Staff performing audits must have right skills and knowledge to assess cloud services effectively
Staff reviewing CSP certifications or audit reports must have right skills and knowledge

sections.source_quote: 41. Considering that cloud services present a high level of technical complexity and raise specific jurisdictional challenges, the staff performing the audit – being the internal auditors of the firm or auditors acting on its behalf – should have the right skills and knowledge to properly assess the relevant cloud services and perform effective and relevant audit. This should also apply to the firms' staff reviewing the certifications or audit reports provided by the CSP.

sections.heading: If sub-outsourcing of critical or important functions (or material parts thereof) is
sections.title: Sub-outsourcing critical functions: contractual requirements
sections.summary: If sub-outsourcing critical functions is permitted, cloud outsourcing agreements must specify excluded sub-outsourcing areas, sub-outsourcing conditions, CSP accountability for sub-outsourced services, notification requirements for sub-outsourcing, and the firm's right to object or approve sub-outsourcing. The agreement must allow time for risk assessment and objection.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires explicit contractual terms for sub-outsourcing critical functions, including notification and objection rights, which are essential for DTIA to assess risks of third-party chain.

requirements.text: Specify parts of outsourced function excluded from sub-outsourcing
Indicate conditions to be complied with in case of sub-outsourcing
Ensure CSP remains accountable for sub-outsourced services
Include obligation for CSP to notify firm of intended sub-outsourcing or material changes
Allow firm sufficient time to conduct risk assessment and object to or approve sub-outsourcing
Ensure firm has right to object to or explicitly approve sub-outsourcing before it takes effect
Ensure firm has right to terminate cloud outsourcing agreement if it objects to sub-outsourcing

sections.source_quote: 42. If sub-outsourcing of critical or important functions (or material parts thereof) is permitted, the cloud outsourcing written agreement between the firm and the CSP should: a) specify any part or aspect of the outsourced function that are excluded from potential sub-outsourcing; b) indicate the conditions to be complied with in case of sub-outsourcing; c) specify that the CSP remains accountable and is obliged to oversee those services that it has sub-outsourced to ensure that all contractual obligations between the CSP and the firm are continuously met; d) include an obligation for the CSP to notify the firm of any intended sub-outsourcing, or material changes thereof, in particular where that might affect the ability of the CSP to meet its obligations under the cloud outsourcing arrangement with the firm. The notification period set in the written agreement should allow the firm sufficient time at least to carry out a risk assessment of the proposed sub-outsourcing or material changes thereof and to object to or explicitly approve them, as indicated in point (e) below; e) ensure that the firm has the right to object to the intended sub-outsourcing, or material changes thereof, or that explicit approval is required before the proposed sub-outsourcing or material changes come into effect; f) ensure that the firm has the contractual right to terminate the cloud outsourcing arrangement with the CSP in case it objects to the proposed sub-outsourcing or material changes thereof and in case of undue sub-outsourcing (for example where the CSP proceeds with the sub-outsourcing without notifying the firm or it seriously infringes the conditions of the sub-outsourcing specified in the outsourcing agreement).

sections.heading: The firm should notify in writing its competent authority in a timely manner of planned
sections.title: Notification of critical cloud outsourcing arrangements
sections.summary: Firms must notify their competent authority in writing of planned cloud outsourcing arrangements for critical or important functions. This also applies to functions previously classified as non-critical that become critical or important.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires firms to inform regulators about critical cloud outsourcing, which is essential for DTIA to track and assess risks of critical functions being outsourced.

requirements.text: Notify competent authority in writing of planned cloud outsourcing arrangements for critical or important functions
Notify competent authority in writing of cloud outsourcing arrangements that become critical or important

sections.source_quote: 44. The firm should notify in writing its competent authority in a timely manner of planned cloud outsourcing arrangements that concern a critical or important function. The firm should also notify in a timely manner and in writing its competent authority of those cloud outsourcing arrangements that concern a function that was previously classified as non-critical or non-important and then became critical or important.

sections.heading: The firm's written notification should include, taking into account the principle of
sections.title: Cloud outsourcing notification content requirements
sections.summary: Firms must include specific information in written notifications to competent authorities about cloud outsourcing arrangements, including start dates, reasons for criticality, CSP details, data locations, risk assessments, and sub-outsourcers. The information must be proportionate and relevant.
sections.dtia_summary: Details required in notifications help DTIA assess risks by providing transparency on data locations, CSPs, and sub-outsourcers, which is critical for cross-border data flows.

requirements.text: Include start date of cloud outsourcing agreement and next renewal date, end date, and notice periods
Provide brief description of outsourced function
State reasons why outsourced function is critical or important
List CSP name, brand, country of registration, corporate registration number, legal entity identifier, registered address, contact details, and parent company
Specify governing law and jurisdiction choice of cloud outsourcing agreement
Detail cloud deployment models and data storage locations
Include date of most recent assessment of criticality or importance
Provide date of most recent risk assessment or audit with summary of results
Identify individual or decision-making body that approved cloud outsourcing
List sub-outsourcers with their country, service location, and data storage locations

sections.source_quote: 45. The firm's written notification should include, taking into account the principle of proportionality, at least the following information: a) the start date of the cloud outsourcing agreement and, as applicable, the next contract renewal date, the end date and/or notice periods for the CSP and for the firm; b) a brief description of the outsourced function; c) a brief summary of the reasons why the outsourced function is considered critical or important; d) the name and the brand name (if any) of the CSP, its country of registration, its corporate registration number, its legal entity identifier (where available), its registered address, its relevant contact details, and the name of its parent company (if any); e) the governing law of the cloud outsourcing agreement and, if any, the choice of jurisdiction; f) the cloud deployment models and the specific nature of the data to be held by the CSP and the locations (namely regions or countries) where such data will be stored; g) the date of the most recent assessment of the criticality or importance of the outsourced function; h) the date of the most recent risk assessment or audit of the CSP together with a brief summary of the main results, and the date of the next planned risk assessment or audit; i) the individual or decision-making body in the firm that approved the cloud outsourcing arrangement; j) where applicable, the names of any sub-outsourcer to which material parts of a critical or important function are sub-outsourced, including the country or region where the sub-outsourcers are registered, where the sub-outsourced service will be performed, and where the data will be stored;

sections.heading: Competent authorities should assess the risks arising from firms' cloud outsourcing
sections.title: Competent authorities assess cloud outsourcing risks
sections.summary: Competent authorities must evaluate risks from firms' cloud outsourcing arrangements during supervision. The assessment specifically targets critical or important functions that are outsourced. This requirement applies to financial institutions under EU regulatory oversight.
sections.dtia_summary: Competent authorities require risk assessments for cloud outsourcing of critical functions, impacting DTIA by mandating specific risk evaluation for cross-border cloud data transfers.

requirements.text: Competent authorities should assess risks arising from firms' cloud outsourcing arrangements
Assessment should focus on arrangements related to outsourcing critical or important functions

sections.source_quote: 46. Competent authorities should assess the risks arising from firms' cloud outsourcing arrangements as part of their supervisory process. In particular, this assessment should focus on the arrangements that relate to the outsourcing of critical or important functions.

sections.heading: Competent authorities should be satisfied that they are able to perform effective
sections.title: Competent authorities ensure effective supervision of outsourced critical functions
sections.summary: Competent authorities must confirm their capacity to supervise effectively when firms outsource critical functions outside the EU. This requirement ensures regulatory oversight remains robust for financial institutions operating across borders.
sections.dtia_summary: Competent authorities must verify supervision effectiveness for outsourced critical functions outside the EU, directly affecting DTIA by requiring cross-border cloud data transfer safeguards.

requirements.text: Competent authorities should be satisfied they are able to perform effective supervision
This applies when firms outsource critical or important functions outside the EU

sections.source_quote: 47. Competent authorities should be satisfied that they are able to perform effective supervision, in particular when firms outsource critical or important functions that are performed outside the EU.

sections.heading: Competent authorities should assess on a risk-based approach whether firms:
sections.title: Competent authorities assess cloud outsourcing governance and risk management
sections.summary: Competent authorities must evaluate firms' governance, resources, and processes for cloud outsourcing on a risk-based approach. They must also ensure firms identify and manage all relevant risks associated with cloud outsourcing activities.
sections.dtia_summary: Competent authorities require firms to implement robust governance and risk management for cloud outsourcing, directly impacting DTIA by mandating specific controls for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Competent authorities should assess whether firms have governance, resources and operational processes to enter, implement, and oversee cloud outsourcing arrangements
Competent authorities should assess whether firms identify and manage all relevant risks related to cloud outsourcing

sections.source_quote: 48. Competent authorities should assess on a risk-based approach whether firms: a) have in place the relevant governance, resources and operational processes to appropriately and effectively enter into, implement, and oversee cloud outsourcing arrangements; b) identify and manage all relevant risks related to cloud outsourcing.

sections.heading: Where concentration risks are identified, competent authorities should monitor the
sections.title: Competent authorities monitor concentration risks from cloud outsourcing
sections.summary: If concentration risks are identified, competent authorities must monitor the development of these risks and evaluate their potential impact on other firms and financial market stability. This applies to cloud outsourcing activities that could affect market concentration.
sections.dtia_summary: Competent authorities must monitor concentration risks from cloud outsourcing, directly affecting DTIA by requiring assessment of cross-border data transfer impacts on market stability.

requirements.text: Competent authorities should monitor development of concentration risks
Competent authorities should evaluate potential impact on other firms and financial market stability

sections.source_quote: 49. Where concentration risks are identified, competent authorities should monitor the development of such risks and evaluate both their potential impact on other firms they supervise and the stability of the financial market.
